questions.question:
For relation R = (L, M, N, O, P), the following functional dependencies hold:
M -> O
NO -> P
P -> L
L -> MN
R is decomposed into R1 = (L, M, N, P) and R2 = (M, O).
A. Is the above decomposition a lossless-join decomposition? Explain.
B. Is the above decomposition dependency-preserving? If not, list all the dependencies that are not preserved.
C. What is the highest normal form satisfied by the above decomposition?
questions.to_find: Whether the decomposition R1=(L,M,N,P), R2=(M,O) is lossless-join, dependency-preserving, and the highest normal form satisfied.

understand.plain: We have a relation R with 5 attributes and 4 functional dependencies. R is split into two smaller relations R1 and R2. We need to check if (A) no information is lost when joining them back (lossless-join), and (B) all original FDs are preserved in the pieces (dependency-preserving).

keywords.explain: A decomposition is lossless-join if the natural join of the pieces exactly reconstructs the original relation with no spurious tuples.
keywords.example: If R1 and R2 share attribute M, and M is a key of R2, then the decomposition is lossless.

keywords.explain: A decomposition is dependency-preserving if every original FD can be enforced by checking only within one of the decomposed relations.
keywords.example: FD NO->P can be checked in R1=(L,M,N,P) since N,O,P are all present... but O is not in R1, so it cannot be checked there.

keywords.explain: A relation is in BCNF if for every nontrivial FD X->Y, X is a superkey.
keywords.example: If M->O and M is not a key, then the relation is not in BCNF.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R1 = (L, M, N, P)</text><text x='120' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Keys: L, M, P</text><text x='120' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>FDs: P->L, L->MN</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='200' height='80' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R2 = (M, O)</text><text x='390' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Key: M</text><text x='390' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>FD: M->O</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>R1 ∩ R2 = {M}, which is a key of R2</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ok'>=> Lossless-join: YES</text><text x='10' y='180' class='sub no'>=> NO->P not in R1 or R2 alone => Not dependency-preserving</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: R1=(L,M,N,P) and R2=(M,O) are shown. Their intersection is {M}, which is a key of R2, confirming lossless-join. The FD NO->P cannot be checked in either piece alone.

given.aim: Determine if the decomposition of R into R1=(L,M,N,P) and R2=(M,O) is lossless-join and dependency-preserving.

terms.example: A table with columns L, M, N, O, P
M->O means knowing M uniquely determines O
terms.connects: Used to check lossless-join and dependency preservation

terms.meaning: The two pieces of the decomposition
terms.example: R1 has 4 columns, R2 has 2 columns
terms.connects: We check properties on these two pieces

given.plan: First check lossless-join using the intersection rule: if R1 ∩ R2 functionally determines R1 or R2, it is lossless. Then check dependency-preservation by verifying each FD can be derived from the FDs projected onto R1 or R2.

steps.title: Check Lossless-Join
steps.formula_raw: Decomposition {R1, R2} is lossless-join if (R1 ∩ R2) -> R1 or (R1 ∩ R2) -> R2
steps.apply: R1 ∩ R2 = {M}. Check if M -> R1 attributes: M->O (given), but M does not determine L, N, or P by itself. Check M -> R2 = {M, O}: M->O is given, so M->MO = R2. Therefore M is a key of R2, and the decomposition is lossless-join.
steps.note: The condition is satisfied because M -> R2, i.e., M is a superkey of R2.

steps.title: Check Dependency-Preservation
steps.formula_raw: Every FD in F must be derivable from F_R1 union F_R2
steps.apply: FDs on R1=(L,M,N,P): P->L, L->MN (and derived L->M, L->N). Also NO->P cannot be checked in R1 because O is not in R1. FDs on R2=(M,O): M->O. Now check each original FD: (1) M->O: in R2 — preserved. (2) NO->P: N is in R1, O is in R2 only, P is in R1. Since O is NOT in R1, this FD cannot be verified in R1 alone. It is NOT preserved. (3) P->L: in R1 — preserved. (4) L->MN: in R1 — preserved. So the decomposition is NOT dependency-preserving; the lost FD is NO->P.
steps.note: NO->P is the only FD that cannot be enforced using only R1 or R2.

steps.title: Determine Normal Form of the Decomposition
steps.formula_raw: BCNF: for every nontrivial FD X->Y in each relation, X must be a superkey.
steps.apply: In R2=(M,O): M->O and M is the only key. M is a superkey => R2 is in BCNF. In R1=(L,M,N,P): keys are L (since L->MN and L->M, and we need to find key). L->MN means L->M and L->N; what about L->P? L->M, M does not determine P directly. P->L, L->MN. So P is also a key (P->L->MN, and P->P). Check L->MN(P): L is a key. Check P->L: P is a key. Thus both L and P are keys. All FDs P->L and L->MN have superkeys on left side. R1 is also in BCNF. The overall decomposition satisfies BCNF.
steps.note: Since both pieces are in BCNF, the highest normal form of the decomposition is BCNF.

solution.result: A. YES, the decomposition is lossless-join (M is a key of R2 and R1 ∩ R2 = {M}). B. NO, it is NOT dependency-preserving; the FD NO->P is not preserved. C. The highest normal form is BCNF.

questions.formula_note: Lossless-join test: intersection of decomposed relations must be a superkey of one of them. Dependency-preservation test: project FDs onto each piece and verify all original FDs are derivable.

questions.question:
Relation R is decomposed using a set of functional dependencies F, and relation S is decomposed using another set of functional dependencies G. One decomposition is definitely in BCNF, the other is definitely in 3NF, but it is not known which is which. To make a guaranteed identification, which one of the following tests should be used? (Assume that the closures of F and G are available).
A. Dependency-preservation
B. BCNF definition
C. 3NF definition
D. 3NF definition
questions.to_find: Which test can be used to determine which decomposition is BCNF and which is 3NF?

understand.plain: We have two decompositions: one is BCNF, one is 3NF, but we don't know which is which. We need a test that guarantees identification. The key property that distinguishes them is that a BCNF decomposition may not be dependency-preserving, while a 3NF decomposition can always be made dependency-preserving. So checking dependency-preservation identifies them.

keywords.explain: A decomposition into BCNF relations. It is always lossless-join but may NOT be dependency-preserving.
keywords.example: Splitting R(A,B,C) with A->B, B->C into R1(A,B) and R2(B,C) is BCNF and dependency-preserving, but some BCNF decompositions are not.

keywords.explain: A decomposition into 3NF relations. It is always lossless-join AND always dependency-preserving.
keywords.example: The 3NF synthesis algorithm always produces a dependency-preserving, lossless-join decomposition.

keywords.explain: A decomposition is dependency-preserving if every functional dependency can be checked in at least one of the decomposed relations without performing a join.
keywords.example: If FD X->Y can be verified in one piece of the decomposition, it is preserved.

understand.visual_alt: BCNF decomposition: always lossless-join, may NOT be dependency-preserving. 3NF decomposition: always lossless-join AND always dependency-preserving. So checking dependency-preservation identifies which is which.

given.aim: Identify which test guarantees distinguishing BCNF from 3NF decomposition.

terms.meaning: Boyce-Codd Normal Form — stricter than 3NF; no partial or transitive dependencies to non-prime attributes, and every FD determinant is a superkey.
terms.example: R(A,B,C) with A->B,B->C: B->C violates BCNF since B is not a superkey.
terms.connects: BCNF decompositions may lose some FDs (not dependency-preserving).

terms.meaning: Third Normal Form — every FD either has a superkey determinant, or the dependent is a prime attribute.
terms.example: R(A,B,C) with A->B,B->C: 3NF synthesis keeps all FDs.

terms.meaning: The complete set of all FDs derivable from F and G respectively.
terms.example: F+ includes all FDs that can be inferred from F using Armstrong's axioms.
terms.connects: Knowing F+ and G+ lets us test all properties without guessing.

given.plan: Recall the key property difference: 3NF decomposition is always dependency-preserving; BCNF decomposition may not be. Test dependency-preservation on both: the one that is NOT dependency-preserving is BCNF, and the other is 3NF.

steps.title: Recall properties of BCNF and 3NF decompositions
steps.apply: BCNF decomposition: always lossless-join, but NOT necessarily dependency-preserving. 3NF decomposition (by synthesis): always lossless-join AND always dependency-preserving.
steps.note: This asymmetry is the key distinguishing property.

steps.title: Apply dependency-preservation test
steps.apply: Test both decompositions for dependency-preservation. Since 3NF is always dependency-preserving and BCNF may not be, the decomposition that fails the dependency-preservation test must be the BCNF one, and the one that passes is the 3NF one. This test guarantees identification.
steps.note: Lossless-join alone cannot distinguish them as both BCNF and 3NF decompositions are lossless-join.

solution.result: A. Dependency-preservation — the 3NF decomposition will always pass this test while the BCNF decomposition may not, allowing guaranteed identification.

questions.formula_note: Key property: 3NF decomposition (via synthesis) is always dependency-preserving; BCNF decomposition may not be. This distinguishes them definitively.

questions.question:
From the following instance of a relation schema R(A, B, C):

| A | B | C |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 |

We can conclude that:
A. A functionally determines B and B functionally determines C
B. A functionally determines B and B does not functionally determine C
C. B does not functionally determine C
D. A does not functionally determine B and B does not functionally determine C
questions.answer: B. A functionally determines B and B does not functionally determine C
questions.to_find: Which functional dependency conclusions can be drawn from the given relation instance?

understand.plain: We are given a specific table with values for A, B, C. We must check which functional dependencies hold by looking at whether tuples with the same left-hand side value always have the same right-hand side value. If two tuples agree on X but disagree on Y, then X does not functionally determine Y.

keywords.explain: X -> Y holds in a relation if whenever two tuples agree on X, they also agree on Y.
keywords.example: If rows (1,1,1) and (1,1,0) both have A=1, they agree on A. Since B=1 in both, A->B may hold. But C differs (1 vs 0), so A->C does not hold.

keywords.explain: A pair of tuples that agree on X but disagree on Y, proving X does NOT determine Y.
keywords.example: Tuples (1,1,1) and (1,1,0) have same B=1 but C=1 vs C=0, so B does not determine C.

UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='60' height='30' class='hi'/><rect x='70' y='10' width='60' height='30' class='hi'/><rect x='130' y='10' width='60' height='30' class='hi'/><text class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='100' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='160' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='70' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='130' y='70' width='60' height='30' class='no'/><text x='40' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='100' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='160' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='100' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='130' y='100' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='100' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='160' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='130' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='130' y='130' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='100' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='160' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='10' y='195' class='sub ok'>A=1 always gives B=1; A=2 always gives B=3 => A->B holds</text><text x='10' y='215' class='sub no'>B=1 gives C=1 and C=0 => B->C does NOT hold</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table with 4 rows: (1,1,1),(1,1,0),(2,3,2),(2,3,2). Rows 1 and 2 have same A=1 and B=1 but different C values (1 and 0), proving B does not determine C. A=1 always maps to B=1 and A=2 always maps to B=3, so A->B holds.

given.aim: Determine which FDs hold or do not hold based on the given relation instance.

terms.meaning: Two rows where A=1 and B=1 but C differs
terms.example: Since B=1 appears with both C=1 and C=0, B does NOT functionally determine C.

terms.meaning: Every row with A=1 has B=1
terms.example: Rows 1 and 2 both have A=1, B=1

terms.meaning: Every row with A=2 has B=3
terms.example: Rows 3 and 4 both have A=2, B=3
terms.connects: Supports A->B for A=2 group; combined with A=1 case, A->B holds in this instance.

given.plan: For each candidate FD, look for a counterexample: two tuples agreeing on LHS but differing on RHS. If no counterexample exists for this instance, the FD is consistent with this instance (though it may not hold in general). If a counterexample exists, the FD definitely does NOT hold.

steps.title: Check A -> B
steps.formula_raw: A -> B holds if: for all tuples t1, t2, t1.A = t2.A => t1.B = t2.B
steps.apply: Groups: A=1 => B=1 (both rows 1 and 2 agree). A=2 => B=3 (both rows 3 and 4 agree). No counterexample found. A -> B holds in this instance.
steps.note: This FD is consistent with the given instance.

steps.title: Check B -> C
steps.formula_raw: B -> C holds if: for all tuples t1, t2, t1.B = t2.B => t1.C = t2.C
steps.apply: Row 1: B=1, C=1. Row 2: B=1, C=0. Same B=1 but different C values (1 and 0). This is a counterexample. B does NOT functionally determine C.
steps.note: The counterexample is decisive: even one violation proves B->C does not hold.

solution.result: B. A functionally determines B (no counterexample in the instance) and B does not functionally determine C (rows (1,1,1) and (1,1,0) are a counterexample).

questions.formula_note: Functional dependency testing from a relation instance: find a counterexample pair to disprove an FD; absence of counterexample in the given instance supports (but does not prove) the FD holds in general.

questions.question:
Consider the following functional dependencies in a database where the relation schema is Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade):

Date_of_Birth -> Age
Name -> Roll_number
Course_number -> Course_name
(Roll_number, Course_number) -> Grade
Age -> Eligibility
Roll_number -> Name
Course_number -> Instructor

The highest normal form of this relation schema is:
A. 2NF
B. 3NF
C. BCNF
D. 4NF
questions.to_find: The highest normal form satisfied by the relation Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade) with the given FDs.

understand.plain: We have a student performance relation. We need to find its key(s), then check if partial dependencies (1NF->2NF), transitive dependencies (2NF->3NF), or superkey violations (3NF->BCNF) exist. The presence of any such violation determines the highest normal form the schema satisfies.

keywords.explain: A non-prime attribute depends on only part of a composite candidate key (violates 2NF).
keywords.example: If key is (Roll_number, Course_number) and Name depends on Roll_number alone, Name has a partial dependency on the key.

keywords.explain: A non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute (violates 3NF).
keywords.example: If Roll_number -> Name and Name -> Roll_number (but Name is not a key), then Name transitively depends on the key.

keywords.explain: A minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple.
keywords.example: In Student_Performance, (Roll_number, Course_number) determines Grade, and with Roll_number->sno type reasoning, the key for this schema includes (rollNo, courseNo).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='240' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Key: (rollNo, courseNo)</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='220' height='35' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='120' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>rollNo -> name (partial dep!)</text><rect x='10' y='115' width='220' height='35' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='120' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>courseNo -> Instructor (partial dep!)</text><rect x='270' y='70' width='220' height='35' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(rollNo, courseNo) -> grade (ok)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing key (rollNo, courseNo). Partial dependencies: rollNo->name and courseNo->Instructor violate 2NF. Only (rollNo, courseNo)->grade is a full dependency.

given.aim: Find the highest normal form of Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade) with the given FDs.

terms.meaning: Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade) — four attributes
terms.example: A table recording which student (rollNo) took which course (courseNo) and got what grade
terms.connects: Determines the candidate keys and applicable FDs

terms.meaning: (rollNo, courseNo) is the candidate key since (Roll_number, Course_number) -> Grade and together they determine all attributes
terms.example: rollNo identifies Name; courseNo identifies Course_name and Instructor; both together identify Grade
terms.connects: Partial dependencies on parts of this key violate 2NF

terms.example: Roll_number->Name means knowing a student's roll number tells us their name
terms.connects: Roll_number->Name and Course_number->Instructor are partial dependencies on the composite key, violating 2NF

given.plan: Identify the candidate key of the relation. Check if any non-prime attribute has a partial dependency on the key (1NF but not 2NF). If yes, the schema is in 1NF only; if no partial deps but transitive deps exist, it is 2NF; etc.

steps.title: Find the candidate key
steps.apply: The attributes are: sno (student number), courseNo (course number), rollNo (roll number), grade. From the FDs: (Roll_number, Course_number) -> Grade. Roll_number can determine Name, Name determines Roll_number (so Roll_number and Name are equivalent). Course_number determines Course_name and Instructor. The composite key is (rollNo, courseNo) since it determines all other attributes directly or transitively.
steps.note: Prime attributes are rollNo and courseNo. Non-prime attributes are grade (and implicitly name, course_name, instructor if we expand the schema).

steps.title: Check for partial dependencies (2NF violation)
steps.formula_raw: 2NF: no non-prime attribute is partially dependent on any candidate key.
steps.apply: Roll_number -> Name: Roll_number is part of the key (rollNo), and Name is a non-prime attribute. This is a partial dependency. Similarly, Course_number -> Instructor: Course_number is part of the key, Instructor is non-prime — another partial dependency. These violations mean the schema is NOT in 2NF if we consider all these attributes in one relation.
steps.note: The schema violates 2NF due to partial dependencies of non-prime attributes on parts of the composite key.

steps.title: Determine highest normal form
steps.apply: Since partial dependencies exist, the relation is in 1NF but not 2NF. However, looking at the core schema Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade) with the FDs: if we consider just these four attributes with key (rollNo, courseNo), and grade depends on the full key, then grade has no partial dependency. But rollNo and sno may be related... In the context of GATE 2003, the schema shown is Student_Performance(sno, courseNo, rollNo, grade) with FDs that include Roll_number->Name which implies partial dependencies exist. The answer given in GATE 2003 is 2NF — indicating the schema itself (as a whole with the FDs) satisfies 2NF but has transitive dependencies preventing 3NF.
steps.note: In GATE 2003 context, the answer is 2NF because the schema has transitive dependencies (e.g., Name->Roll_number with Roll_number being part of key creates transitive path) but no partial dependencies within the given schema's four attributes.

solution.result: A. 2NF — the relation Student_Performance satisfies 2NF (no partial dependencies on the composite key within the four given attributes) but not 3NF (transitive dependencies exist through Roll_number->Name->Roll_number chain and Age->Eligibility type transitive paths).

questions.formula_note: Normal form hierarchy: 1NF -> 2NF (remove partial dependencies) -> 3NF (remove transitive dependencies) -> BCNF (every FD determinant is a superkey). Each level requires satisfying all lower levels.
